sentences.text: Cohen.
Aaron Moses Cohen.
A-A-R-O-N, initial M, C-O-H-E-N.
My given name in birth was Aaron [NON-ENGLISH] Cohen.
My birthday is December 18, 1929.
I was born in a city called Gorna Dzhumaya.
Shall I spell it for you?
Gorna Dzhumaya, in the country of Bulgaria.
[SIGHS] To tell you about my childhood, I have to briefly tell you about Bulgaria and the Jews that lived in Bulgaria.
I can remember as a child-- and I would say that that was a childhood of innocence-- I remember four years old, I was.
That's the first recollection.
In fact, I have a recollection of me sitting on a high chair, and having my great-grandfather visit me, very, very vaguely.
And I was shaking very hard on the-- on the high chair.
I was a year and a half, two years.
That was my first recollection.
Then I blank out until I am about four.
And I remember my mother taking me for a walk and-- in-- in the city, lovely city.
The Jews enjoyed very, very fine reputation.
My-- my family on my father's side, which were residing at that time in Gorna Dzhumaya, enjoyed excellent reputation.
They were merchants.
And primarily, they dealt in furs, tobacco, and jewelry.
That was their-- their primary occupation.
And to give you the difference between my family, on my father's side, they were real strong, tough individuals.
It was-- the-- the town was bordering Macedonia.
And one of the reasons my father had to leave Gorna Dzhumaya, because the Macedonians wanted him to become their secretary-treasurer of that party.
And they were very tough.
I remember my mother saying that the black pickup-- if the black pickup, which was covered, would pass through the street, they knew that they had somebody that black-- black pickup to be hung.

So, my father, because he had friends in any party of the Bulgarian government, did not want to get involved in one particular party, not to incur neither the wrath or to incur the displeasure of-- of friends that he grew up with.
So rather than do that, he moved to Plovdiv, which is the second largest city in Bulgaria.
And that's where my family on my mother's side resided.
On my mother's side, they were very, very fine, more of the scholarly type people.
My-- one of my uncles was the first chairman of the board of the bank of-- Franco-Bulgarian Bank.
Now, that was-- if you-- have to understand, that was in Bulgaria.
And that was in the 1930s.
And it was a very, very coveted position.
In fact, some of the resentment on my father's side was that I was not in that city as the nephew of David, rather than the son of Moses.
But my other uncle was a businessman.
He had properties.
He had a hotel there.
He had a-- a store for men's clothing, the finest, on the main street.
He had several houses.
And my youngest uncle, which was really my-- my favorite uncle on my mother's side, he was a pharmacist.
And he, finally, when he got his own pharmacy, then the-- the Nazis came.
And then it really broke his heart that they had to confiscate his pharmacy.
[DRINKS]
His name was Moses, but they called him Moshe.
In fact, his grandfather gave him an Arabic name, Turkish name, which called him Musa.
In fact, in Bulgaria right now, the highest peak of the mountain is called Musala, which means the mountain of Musa, which is the prophet Moses, and that was next to heaven, which is Allah.
So it still has that name, Musala.
But they-- he would call him Musa.
My father was educated in Turkey.
And to talk about Bulgaria, you have to talk a bit about Turkey.
I'm sure you know, Cydney, that the Jews of Spain were expelled in 1492.

And the sultan, I believe it was [INAUDIBLE], sent his flotilla to Europe, to pick up the remnants of the Jews, that they're languishing on the shores of Italy, France, Europe, all over, and brought into Turkey.
And if you follow history, you will see that Ottoman Empire benefit greatly by their industriousness.
And also, the Jews enjoyed freedom, enjoyed very good conditions, and they were able to immediately found their own schools.
So they-- they-- they had schools for rabbis.

And-- and in fact, if you go a little inland after that, you see that one of the most important religious-- religious books that was written by Rabbi Karo, was written in Bulgaria in the 16th century in the city of Nikopol.
Not to divert myself so much from my father.
My father--
My father was a very strong, handsome man, and very enterprising.
Even though he had to leave properties and everything, sort of, to leave in a hurry and to come back to Plovdiv, he had extreme pride in him.
He did not want the-- my uncles on my mother's side to situate him or to find him a job.
So he found a job on his own.
And he became a representative, like a sales executive, for a very large company.
And the funniest thing I remember about him was that when I was eight years old, he took me with him on a half a day trip into the country.
In other-- in other words, he was doing his job, but he still had me accompany him.
And subsequently, I really picked up some very interesting virtues from him.
If he had to take the conventional, which was a train that will pass in the middle of the night through-- through Plovdiv, he could never afford to be late.
So they had carriages with horses those days.
And let's say that the fare would be $20, which was with the tip and all.
He would take a $100 bill, tear it in the middle.
He'll give to the coachman, one half, he says, you pick me up at midnight.
You'll have the other half, and another $20 for your tip.
So he never missed the train.
And another thing I learned from him was being that they lost fortunes during First World War and during uprisings and during the wars, and one government comes, another government they had to flee.
Whatever they did, he always told me, because they handled gold, so there-- they liked to keep a lot of their property ready to move.
And they have that in gold, in round money, in the round-- in the round coins.
And he would always tell me, coins are round.
One day are with you, and the other day, they run away from you.
They roll.
But your word of honor never leaves you.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he taught me a great deal about honor and-- and the-- the ethics, being on time.
And 42 years' time in-- in Los Angeles, I've never been late for an appointment in my life.
In fact, as you know, I was here 45 minutes early.
So--
Yes.
In--
The--
Oh, the Macedonians were a party at that time.
And they tried to get to be independent.
And as you know, Macedonia from-- Alexander from Macedonia, it was a very prominent area in the Balkans.
And those were Bulgarians.
And they are-- it-- it-- it's-- Macedonia is situated between Bulgaria, Greece, and Yugoslavia.
So they were struggling for their independence, and especially the Bulgarian Macedonians.
And they're known to be very, very tough hombres.
Very tough individuals.
And their party was to the point that they have-- they are very strong-armed.
If somebody opposed them, and-- they would pick him up in the middle of the night, with a kangaroo trial, and within 24 hours, they'll hang him.
My father had very good friends in among the Macedonians.
And he used to call them [NON-ENGLISH], which means the-- the Jewish young man.
It's not a bad connotation.
It's not like-- like-- it's not a bad word.
[NON-ENGLISH].
So, [NON-ENGLISH], he wants to be our secretary-treasurer.
Well, he had other friends in other parties.
So, not that he didn't want to be in the middle.
He always took sides.
But this is one, it was a very sensitive issue.
And they had great confidence in him because he knew how to handle himself as well as handle money.
And they trusted him.
So this is when he left.
Now on my-- on my-- on my father's side, you have to understand, Cydney, there were 13 children.
My father came after six sisters.
So, my mother was a very wise woman.
She found that in the Sephardic tradition, when the woman is pregnant, they treat her like a queen.
So she stayed pregnant most of her life.
She had 13 children.
And very fortunately, they did not die at birth.
So they did not die.
Of all the children, I remember my father speaking of one of his favorite sisters that passed away, and how he would go to temple and pray for her while she was sick.
And he became very bitter after he-- he lost that sister.
I remember that, the-- telling me.
Going back to the-- to the-- it was called in-- in Bulgarian, [BULGARIAN].
So when my mother would see it, they would shudder, because they were always afraid maybe some of their friends would be.
Because they were friends and they were liberal.
And some of them, they're socialist.
And some of them were also at that time, with-- with other parties, very liberal.
And she hated to see anyone-- any harm come to anyone.
She was a very, very fine, fine, saintly woman.
Extremely intelligent.
But then when my father-- he was very young.
He was handsome.
And the whole family on-- on my father's side are dark, Mediterranean-type.
Whereby-- I don't know why.
It's in the same country.
But on my mother's side, they all were very fair.
My mother had practically blond hair, very fair hair, and fair, white skin, like-- like ivory.
And all my uncles, the same way.
And here, you have the contradiction of my father, dark, my mother, very light.
My brother looks and has the skin of my mother.
And I have the skin of my father.
My father political affiliation, primarily affiliated with Jewish causes.
As far as affiliation with-- with-- in-- in Bulgarian parties, I don't recall exactly what affiliation he was.
But he was rather conservative.
And-- but he was very, very closely affiliated with the Zionist organization.
He was affiliated with-- with the masons there.
And he was affiliated with an organization that-- it was like a commercial group of traveling executives.
He did a lot of traveling for the company.
And [DRINKS] my recollection was, he would take me on a half a day trip when I was eight.
When I was nine, he took me on a full-day trip.
And when-- that was it on a train.
And then he took me on a two-day trip, overnight.
And that was very exciting.
And then after that, all of a sudden, like I said, that was the-- the end of the dream, of the-- of the innocence.
Like, the bad winds began to blow from Europe, hearing about the Nazis beginning to occupy.
And I remember, I was at age 10, and we are standing in the high holidays in our temple, Zion.
And we had two rabbis.
We used to call them [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] Shmuel and [NON-ENGLISH].
And both of them were expounding at that time.

And you have chosen us, of all people, and my father very quietly-- and, have to understand, Cydney, in those days, the edict had just come out in defense of the Bulgarian nation, which meant that they've taken all our properties.
They had restricted our movement.
My father lost his job.
They closed the stores.
They moved my uncles away from their homes, which were beautiful homes.
That was 1941. 1941.

Because in-- that's 1941, when the edict came out, and we're sitting in temple, and the Nazis are advancing, and my father-- when the rabbi sang, and you have chosen them from all the people, my father very quietly said, well, choose somebody else already.
[LAUGHS] But, be as it may, he had very strong-- strong beliefs that everything will turn out OK.
And those are the days when he-- he really began to-- to get active with me, to make sure that I would be able to leave the country.
And this is when I got involved with the [INAUDIBLE].
And through the [INAUDIBLE], it took me two years to get my papers in order.
And then through the [INAUDIBLE], we came to-- to Palestine at that time.
Let-- Let's talk a little bit more about your prewar experience.
Tell me about your religious background.
What was that like for you, growing up?
It was wonderful.
Our religious background was actually, we are Orthodox.
But in a sense, we're conservative.
The reason I'm saying we were Orthodox, because in temple, the women would sit separately from the men.
But as far as the rest of the-- the-- the way, it was very, very progressive.
The whole country was very progressive.
The Jews were very progressive.
But our faith, if I remember very, very well, I remember, is I was only five years old, 5 and 1/2 years old, when my mother and my father enrolled me into Hebrew school.
So we went to-- into kindergarten there.
In fact, I just brought you a picture to see that we had a play about Purim.
And since we were children, we were in-- there was instilled in us that sense of Jewishness, and Judaism, and love for-- for our people, and love for our history.
And this is why my father chose to put me in Hebrew school, rather than, like, my other cousins went into a French college.
It was called-- private French schools.
And I-- speaking because of my father's very strong belief that that's what we should do.
Now, in the Sephardic way there, the children, since they were small, they were given sort of an autonomy.
Meaning that when we would go to temple, right at the schoolyard of the synagogue, there would be also a small building, which was called Beta Midrash.
And in the Sephardic tradition, usually the pulpit is in the middle, or toward the end.
And the rabbi is surrounded by all the people.
And we had the pulpit in the middle.
And we had an assistant rabbi that would be with us.
But we would be conducting those services ourselves.
Friday night and Saturday morning.
And we'll go-- you couldn't go to the Torah.
But we would read, you know, parts of the scripture, without being bar mitzvahed.
Because I was never bar mitzvahed.
By the time my time came for a bar mitzvah, the Germans were there.
They-- they took the schools.
They took the-- the area.
They took the temple.
They took Beta Midrash.
And we just didn't have anything.
Actually, any age.
We were from-- from fourth grade, third grade, through to the junior high.
The seniors, you know, the-- we went seven grades.
Four grades were the-- what do you call that, the-- four grade school.
And then you went three years into the junior high school.
Like public schools.
But we studied everything in Hebrew and the same subject we study in Bulgarian.
So after that, a commission, we had to pass a test in front of a commission of the education department.
We always passed it with flying colors, even though we were taxed, studying both subjects, most of the subjects in Hebrew, as well as in English.
It was not like in America when kids go to cheder, and after the age of 13, luckily if they continue after that.
And I hope my grandkids continue after that.
But in-- in Bulgaria, you spoke Hebrew fluently.
You-- you spoke it.
You-- you conversed with it.
When teachers came into the room, they spoke to you in Hebrew.
So the subject [NON-ENGLISH], the history of our-- of-- of our people, we studied in Hebrew.
Tanakh, the Torah, we studied in Hebrew.
But we did not study it phonetically here, just to remember it by memory.
We knew.
I can recite to you today, like, when the-- Isaiah.
Can tell you right now, very quickly, [HEBREW].
That's 60 years ago, 58 years ago.
Yes.
Very little, unfortunately.
Very, very little.
I was fortunate to spend only one summer with my paternal grandmother.
I always resented the fact that I had no grandparents.
By the time I was born, my maternal grandparents had passed away.
And my grandfather had passed away.
Although I very, very vaguely remember my great-grandfather, that we called affectionately Papuchi.
Bulgarian.
Bulgarian.
Very well-educated, my mother was a teacher.
She was one of the first ladies to-- to graduate high school at those days, which was very, very progressive.
And then--
Esther.
Esther.
Interesting thing is that whenever my kid brother and I, that lives in Israel-- his name is Amnon-- whenever we get together, we always talk about my father.
Because our father was a character.
But my mother, how much can we talk about a saint, you know?
She had extreme, extreme, sharp mind, extremely sharp mind.
I'll give you an example, Cydney.
She came to America.
We brought them here in 1962.
And the first thing she went, she went to-- to school to study English.
As soon as she mastered English, very quickly, within three, four, five months, she enrolled into high school.
She graduated high school and she enrolled into UCLA.
And then at that time, she fell ill.
And unfortunately, she passed away very, very young, 63.
But she always studied.
And she was a voracious reader.
And through her, until today, I'm-- I'm-- I owe that to her.
I'm a voracious reader.
I read a lot.
She was-- she had very deep conviction.
But she was not the sort of Orthodox woman.
We did keep kosher at home.
We observed the traditions.
But she was not fanatic.
None of-- of-- of the population at that time were in any way Orthodox to the point they were fanatics, really.
They were very progressive.
They were conservative.
They observed very, very-- they adhered very, very closely to the Jewish faith.
One thing that sticks in my mind, Cydney, when you begin to speak about the-- the Jewish faith there, I began to tell you that we had the-- the play of Purim.
My mother, god rest her soul-- [DRINKS] she was a diabetic.
But during Purim, she fasted.
[CRYING]
We always tried to talk her out of it, you know?
Because it was so harmful to her health.
But she observed-- it was called Ta'anit Esther, in honor-- [SIGHS] excuse me.
It was in honor of the queen Esther that save our people.
And she always observed that.
This is one thing.
And I-- I remember distinctly how my mother spoke with great respect for a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very coveted position of three women in-- in-- in-- in Plovdiv.
They were called rochetze.
It comes from the Hebrew word rochetz, which means to wash.
Those women were like, respected like they walk on air.
They used to prepare the body for burial.
They would wash the body, anoint it, and get it ready.
And of course, in those days, you put it-- you know, that's what they did.
That was a very coveted position.
Funerals, I remember distinctly that it was strictly men by volunteers.
And those volunteers took great pride in what they were doing.
And I was just taught to be very respectful to these people.
Like, we had a very poor man that used to run-- he used to be ahead of the procession.
And he would be just a small merchant that would be sell something, you know, like, from a-- from a carriage.
But when the time for the funeral, he don a-- a uniform.
And he was carrying a, like a light, like a lantern.
You know?
And we'd be ahead of the procession, which was a carriage drawn by horses with glass on all sides.
So this was one occasion when-- when the Nazis and the-- particularly the fascists in Bulgaria, allowed us to go to the cemeteries.

So we all used, as children, the opportunity even if somebody old, or whoever died, always to be at the funeral, so we can use the opportunity to go out and get some fresh air, get out of the right-- not the ghetto.
We did not have a ghetto, per se.
But to the-- the confined areas in which we were confined.
We could not walk-- we could not walk to the main streets.
We could not walk through many other areas during the-- the fascists and the Nazis.
Well, I really don't know if they were Sephardic.
But that's the way I grew up with.
That was the Sephardic tradition.
Passover was incredible.
We would gather the whole family at my uncle's house that day.
They lived right next to the station.
It was the most beautiful street.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was the gentleman that was the-- the chairman of the board of the bank, the president of the bank.
And he had-- I can't forget this-- the-- there was such a beautiful place.
Parquet floors with thick Persian rugs.
I've never seen rugs that thick, even when I just went to Turkey.
And the whole family would gather together.
And they would go through that Seder in Ladino.
In Ladino and in Hebrew.
And we-- we sat there.
Let me tell you, there was no fooling around.
We were very precocious, of course.
And the-- the minute we began to move a little bit around, there was one look.
My father never touched me.
My parents never touched me.
There was no such thing as discipline.
But one look from them, we knew what to do.
We knew-- we knew what not to do.
The reason I wanted to elaborate a little bit more on-- on my mother, her affiliation, there was a very fine Jewish organization affiliated with Zionism.
It was called WIZO.
And my mother went-- she was, of course, raised in Plovdiv, but when my father married her, they went back to his city, Gorna Dzhumaya.
And she founded the chapter of WIZO in Gorna Dzhumaya.
And she was not a woman libber.
It's not like today that it's so much in vogue.
But she was in the women's suffrage, you know, foreground.
When it was unheard of, she went to school, she graduated high school, she-- she took her teaching credential.
She studied.
She organized.
She was always super social.
And if I have any appreciation of fine art, and music, literature, I owe it to her.
Because as my father would be traveling for weeks at a time abroad, I would be really going with her to all these fine things with her, with her sisters.
So I grew up in this kind of atmosphere from day one.
